documents.source: T . G. Edwards, manager of a government-run sugar factory in W onopringgo, Java, Dutch East Indies, letter to the Dutch colonial government in Jakarta, 1858. Unfortunately, many of the potential Javanese workers for the sugar processing factory are already f
documents.content:
orced to
work on sugar fields under the Dutch government's Cultivation System.1 There is not a single peasant in the
district who is not subject to multiple demands on his labor, from the government or from local Javanese
elites.
I have had one of my factory agents travel around the villages in the district all year looking for workers.
Despite offering them good wages, I have never succeeded in getting more than five men per day. W hen I ask
the men to work in the factory full time, they all answer that they would if I could get them freed from
government-imposed work.
A system of labor in Dutch-ruled Java that forced Javanese farmers to work for parts of the year producing export cash crops, which they then had to sell to
the colonial government at artificially low prices

documents.source: The British East India Company, data from reports submitted to the British Parliament, based on totals of all goods traded in Indian ports that were under the control of the company, 1810–1830. 8
Bankim Chandra Chatterjee, Indian journalist and intellectual,
documents.content:
The Cultivators of Bengal, article
published in a Bengali-language newspaper, Kolkata, India, 1872.
We pay for what we buy from England by exporting Indian agricultural commodities such as rice, silk,
indigo, etc. It goes without saying that as the trade with England expands, so will the demand for such
agricultural commodities. Ever since the establishment of British rule, the trade of India has increased,
leading to an expansion of agriculture.
The Indian cotton weaving trade may have collapsed because of cheap British cloth imports, but why does
the weaver not move to another occupation? He may not be able to support his family by weaving cloth, but
he should be able to do so if he would switch to cultivating rice.
But people in our country are reluctant to give up their hereditary trades. T his reluctance is unfortunate for
our weavers, but it does not mean a loss of wealth for India as a whole.

documents.source: Lobengula Khumalo, ruler of the Matabele (Ndebele) people of present-day Zimbabwe, contract with business associates of English businessman Cecil Rhodes' m ining company, 1888. Let it be known that Charles Rudd of Kimberley (South
documents.content:
A frica), Rochfort Maguire of London, and Francis
Thompson of Kimberley have made a contract with me and agreed to pay me and my heirs the monthly sum
of 100 pounds sterling (British currency) and the delivery of 1,000 rifles made in England. Further, they have
promised to deliver a steamboat with guns suitable for the defense of my territories on the Zambezi River.
In exchange for these presents, I hereby grant to the above-mentioned individuals and their heirs the complete
and exclusive right to collect, sell, and enjoy the profits of all of the metals and minerals contained in my
kingdoms. A nd since I have been much pestered lately by various persons and companies seeking these
rights, I further grant to them the right to take all necessary measures to exclude all their competitors seeking
mining rights and privileges from my kingdoms.

documents.source: Moyo, a Congolese refugee, testimony given to the Belgian government as part of a larger investigation into the condition of the native population of the Belgian Congo, 1904. Each village from our district had to produce 80 loads of rubber per month
documents.content:
A s rubber plants got scarcer, the
White man reduced the required amount only by a little. W e got no pay! Our village got cloth and a little salt
from the government, but it did not go to the people who collected the rubber. Instead, our chiefs used up the
cloth; the workers got nothing. T he pay was given to the Chief, never to the men.
It used to take ten days to get twenty loads of rubber. W e were always in the forest and then if we were late
making the delivery, we could be killed. W e had to go further into the forest to find the rubber vines and our
women had to give up cultivating fields and gardens. T hen we starved. W ild animals killed some of us when
we were working in the forest, and others got lost or died from exposure and starvation. W e begged the W hite
men, saying we could get no more rubber, but the W hite men and their soldiers refused. W e tried, always, to
go further into the forest, and when we failed, and our rubber delivery was short, the soldiers came to our
towns.

documents.source: N dansi Kumalo, member of the Ndebele ethnic group of present-day Zimbabwe, oral memoir of his experiences in the 1890s, recorded by a British anticolonial activist and published in 1936. After our rebellion had been put down,1 we were offered work in the mines and farms of the W hite pe
documents.content:
ople to
earn money, and so we were able to buy back some cattle to replace the ones that had been lost during the
rebellion. A t first, of course, we were not used to working for a wage, but the colonial government ordered
the chiefs to advise the young people to go to work, and gradually they went. In a few years we had
recovered our livelihoods somewhat.
But then the taxes came. A t first, it was 10 shillings [British currency] a year. Soon the Government said,
"This is too little, you must contribute more, you must pay one pound." W e were also taxed 5 shillings for a
dog. T hen the Government told us that we were living on private land that supposedly belonged to the W hite
settlers; the owners wanted rent in addition to the Government tax.
a reference to the 1896–1897 Ndebele revolt against W hite settlers

documents.source: Anonymous T anzanian participant in the 1905–1907 Maji Maji uprising against German colonial rule, interview conducted by A frican historians from newly independent T anzania, published in 1967
documents.content:
In 1902, the Germans established cotton plantations in our Matumbi district. Every village was allotted days
on which it had to cultivate the plantations. One person had to come from each household on the allotted
days.
This work made us suffer greatly. W e were whipped for the smallest mistake, and once you started working,
there was no break. Some of us were assigned to clear the land of trees, others tilled the land, others would
smooth the ground and plant the cotton seeds, another group did the weeding, another the picking, and yet
another transported the bales of cotton to the coast beyond Kikanda for shipping.1 The work was
astonishingly hard and our only reward was the whip. A nd yet the German also wanted us to pay him taxes in
addition to the plantation work! Our people came to hate German rule, which was so cruel. It was not
because we were lazy or disliked agriculture. If it had been good agriculture with meaning and profit, we
would never have risked our lives by starting a rebellion.
The colonial government of German East A frica exported the cotton to Europe.
END OF DOCUMENTS FOR QUESTION 1

parts.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which European imperialism affected economies in A frica and/or A sia in the nineteenth
and early twentieth centuries.
parts.scoring_notes: Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence from Documents (2), Evidence Beyond Documents (1), Sourcing (1), Complexity (1). Total: 7 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis (1 pt): Makes a historically defensible claim that establishes a line of reasoning about the extent to which European imperialism affected economies in Africa and/or Asia in the 19th and early 20th centuries. Must do more than restate the prompt.

required_elements: Defensible claim about extent
Line of reasoning connecting imperialism to specific economic outcomes
alternatives.correct_example: European imperialism fundamentally restructured the economies of Africa and Asia from diversified subsistence and artisanal production toward export-commodity dependence, as colonial powers used coerced labor systems (Doc 1, Doc 5, Doc 7) and favorable trade policies to extract raw materials — though the degree of structural disruption varied depending on the pre-existing economic complexity of the colonized region and the specific form of colonial control imposed.

required_elements: Defensible claim about extent
Identifies specific mechanisms of economic impact
alternatives.correct_example: While European imperialism brought expanded trade volumes and new economic opportunities to certain elites in Africa and Asia (Doc 3), its dominant economic impact was to destroy existing manufacturing industries, coerce indigenous labor through tribute and taxation systems, and channel wealth to European metropoles — creating a pattern of underdevelopment that would persist long after formal decolonization.

wrong_examples: European imperialism affected the economies of Africa and Asia in many ways. (Restatement — no line of reasoning or extent argument)
Imperialism was bad for Africa and Asia. (Evaluative claim without a line of reasoning about mechanism or extent)
common_traps: Writing a thesis that describes what the documents say rather than making an argument about extent
Claiming imperialism was either entirely destructive or entirely beneficial without acknowledging variation
Placing the thesis only in the conclusion rather than the introduction
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning. The thesis or claim must provide a historically defensible thesis or claim that establishes a position on the extent to which European imperialism affected economies in Africa and/or Asia in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The thesis or claim must either provide some indication of the reason for making that claim OR establish categories of the argument. The thesis must consist of one or more sentences located in one place, either in the introduction or the conclusion.

sample_responses.response: States in Africa and Asia often saw their global trade heavily disrupted by European imperialism.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of a thesis that earns the point — establishes a line of reasoning that evaluates the topic of the prompt by taking a position on the extent to which imperialism disrupted African and Asian trade.

sample_responses.response: While European Imperialism certainly forced African and Asian economies to change, these changes often benefited local elites and Europeans.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — establishes a line of reasoning with analytic categories (forced change + differential winners) that evaluates extent.

sample_responses.response: Europeans imported raw materials from Asia and Africa.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of a response that does NOT earn the point — provides a historically defensible claim but does not establish a line of reasoning or take a position on extent.

sample_responses.response: The First and Second World War strongly influenced anti-colonial efforts in Africa and Asia.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of a response that does NOT earn the point — does not focus on the topic of the prompt (economic impact of imperialism).

scoring_points.description: Contextualization (1 pt): Accurately describes a broader historical context relevant to the prompt and explains how it connects to the argument. Must be more than a brief mention — requires a developed explanation of the connection.

required_elements: Broader historical context from before or beyond the prompt's period
Explicit connection to the argument about imperial economic impact
alternatives.correct_example: The Industrial Revolution in Britain (beginning ~1760s) transformed the nature of European imperialism by creating an insatiable demand for raw materials to feed factories and markets for finished manufactured goods — fundamentally altering the economic logic of colonial extraction. This shift from mercantilist trade-post empires to industrial-era colonial economies set the context for the documents in this DBQ: the Dutch Cultivation System (Doc 1), the Belgian rubber quota (Doc 5), and the German cotton plantation (Doc 7) all reflect the industrial-era demand for specific raw commodities, explaining why the economic disruption of 19th-century imperialism was far more invasive and systematically destructive than earlier forms of colonial trade.

required_elements: Broader context explaining preconditions of 19th-century imperialism
Connection to the economic argument
alternatives.correct_example: The Scramble for Africa (1880s–1914), formalized through the Berlin Conference of 1884–85, marked the shift from informal to formal European imperialism as industrialized powers partitioned the African continent for economic exploitation. This context explains why Docs 4–7 all date from 1888 onward and reflect systematic colonial extraction rather than individual merchant activities: the Berlin Conference created the framework under which European states claimed exclusive exploitation rights, setting the legal and political foundation for the labor coercion and economic restructuring visible across the documents.

wrong_examples: European countries wanted to build empires for economic reasons. (Circular — describes the prompt topic, not a broader context)
Before imperialism, Africa and Asia had their own economies. (Too vague — no connection explaining how the pre-imperial context shaped the specific economic impacts in the documents)
common_traps: Describing the content of the documents themselves as the context — contextualization must come from outside the documents
Providing a one-sentence mention of a broader event without explaining how it shaped the specific argument
Using events from within the prompt's own period as the context
scoring_points.official_rubric: Describes a broader historical context relevant to the prompt. Must accurately describe a context relevant to the extent to which European imperialism affected economies in Africa and/or Asia in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. CB-listed acceptable contexts include: European extraction economies (e.g., mining operations in Africa); European nations competing with one another for influence and territory in Africa and Asia; anti-imperialist movements influenced by nationalism (e.g., Thailand); European infrastructure projects in imperialized regions (e.g., British railroads in India); fossil fuel demand driving industrial raw material needs; new imperialist ideas such as the civilizing mission and Social Darwinism. The context must be more than a phrase or reference — appropriate elaboration required.

sample_responses.response: European nations in the late nineteenth century divided up Africa between them during the Berlin Conference.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of acceptable contextualization — names a specific event (Berlin Conference, 1884–85) that established the framework for European economic partition of Africa, and with appropriate elaboration this connects to the economic impact argument.

sample_responses.response: States during the First World War produced propaganda to convince soldiers to fight.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — does not provide context relevant to the topic of the prompt (economic impact of imperialism in Africa/Asia).

sample_responses.response: European Imperialism often perpetuated racial prejudice.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — provides only a passing phrase or reference without developed elaboration connecting racial ideology to the economic argument.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Documents — 3 Documents (1 pt): Uses the content of at least three documents to address the topic of European imperialism's economic effects. Must accurately describe what the documents say — not just cite them.

required_elements: Accurate description of content from at least three documents
All relevant to the economic impact of European imperialism
alternatives.correct_example: Doc 1 describes how the Dutch Cultivation System in Java compelled peasants to work on government sugar fields, creating a labor shortage that prevented factory managers from recruiting free workers even at competitive wages. Doc 5 details how Belgian Congo rubber quotas forced Congolese villagers to spend increasing time in the forest collecting rubber, abandoning food cultivation and leading to starvation. Doc 7 describes how German mandatory cotton plantation labor in Tanganyika was enforced through physical violence, with workers receiving no payment beyond whip strokes.

required_elements: Accurate content from at least three documents
Used to address the prompt topic
alternatives.correct_example: Doc 3 shows an Indian intellectual arguing that while British colonialism destroyed the Indian weaving industry, expanded trade offered alternative agricultural livelihoods — suggesting the economic disruption was partial or replaceable. Doc 4 reveals how Ndebele ruler Lobengula granted Cecil Rhodes' company exclusive mineral rights in exchange for rifles and money, showing how African leaders sometimes negotiated with imperial economic demands. Doc 6 describes how post-rebellion Ndebele people were compelled to take wage labor and then subjected to escalating colonial taxes that extracted the wages they earned.

wrong_examples: The documents show that European imperialism had negative effects on African and Asian economies. (Paraphrasing without accurately describing specific content from three identified documents)
Doc 1 talks about labor issues and Doc 5 talks about rubber. (Too vague — must accurately describe the content, not just identify the topic)
common_traps: Citing document numbers without accurately describing their content
Misattributing one document's content to another document number
Describing only two documents accurately and mentioning a third only vaguely
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses the content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt. The response must accurately describe — rather than simply quote — the content from at least three of the documents to address the topic of European imperialism and/or economies in Africa and/or Asia in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

sample_responses.response: (Document 2): The graph shows that there was a big reversal in the trade between 1810 and 1830, with India exporting less cloth to Britain and importing much more cloth from Britain.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of describing the content of a document relevant to the topic — accurately describes Doc 2's trade-reversal data relevant to imperial economic impact on India.

sample_responses.response: (Document 5): The account by the Congolese refugee shows how the Belgian colonizers' harsh demands for rubber production led to starvation and violence among the native population.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — accurately describes Doc 5's content (Congolese refugee account) in a way that addresses the prompt's topic of imperial economic impact.

sample_responses.response: The documents mention many problems.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague reference to 'the documents' without describing specific content from three identified documents — fails the CB standard of accurately describing document content.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Documents — 4 Documents Supporting Argument (1 pt): Uses the content of at least four documents to support an argument in response to the prompt. Document content must be explicitly linked to a specific claim about the extent of economic impact.

required_elements: Content from at least four documents
Explicitly linked to an argument about the extent of European imperialism's economic impact
alternatives.correct_example: The documents collectively support the argument that the primary mechanism of imperial economic impact was forced labor and coerced extraction rather than market integration. Doc 1 shows the Cultivation System eliminating free labor markets by compelling Javanese farmers into government work. Doc 5 shows Belgian rubber quotas forcing Congolese communities to abandon subsistence agriculture entirely. Doc 7 shows German plantation labor enforced by violence with no compensation. Taken together, these three documents demonstrate that labor coercion — not simply trade or market competition — was the dominant economic mechanism of imperialism. Doc 6 adds a further dimension by showing how wage labor, initially adopted voluntarily by the Ndebele after their defeat, was then rendered coercive through escalating colonial taxes that compelled continued labor to pay the imposed fees — demonstrating the interconnection of tax policy and labor coercion as tools of economic extraction.

required_elements: Four documents used to support a specific argument
Argument about the extent or nature of economic impact
alternatives.correct_example: The documents support a nuanced argument that European imperialism's economic effects depended significantly on pre-existing African/Asian power structures. Where indigenous leaders retained political leverage — as shown by Lobengula's negotiation in Doc 4, where he secured rifles and money as conditions for granting mineral rights — the economic impact involved negotiation as well as extraction. Where political resistance had already been crushed — as in Doc 6, where post-rebellion Ndebele were forced into wage labor, and Doc 5, where Congolese communities had no recourse against rubber quotas — the economic impact was purely extractive. Doc 3 further complicates the picture by showing that Indian elites with access to the new agricultural commodity economy could adapt — even if Indian artisanal workers could not — suggesting that class position within colonized societies shaped the economic experience of imperialism.

wrong_examples: All seven documents show that imperialism was economically harmful. (Does not use specific content from four documents to support a specific argument — too general)
Docs 1, 5, and 7 show coerced labor. (Only three documents used to support the argument)
common_traps: Summarizing documents without explicitly connecting them to a specific argument
Using four documents but only three specifically — the fourth must be substantively integrated, not just mentioned
Making an argument and then listing documents as examples without explaining how each document's specific content supports the claim
scoring_points.official_rubric: Supports an argument in response to the prompt using at least six documents (CB 2022 standard, combined evidence row). The response must accurately use the content of at least six documents to support an argument in response to the prompt. The documents do not have to be used in support of a single argument — they can be used across sub-arguments or to address counterarguments.

sample_responses.response: (Document 5): The account by the Congolese refugee shows how the Belgian colonizers' harsh demands for rubber production led to starvation and violence among the native population. The account shows the extent of economic disruption brought to African society by imperialism. (Connects the content of the document to an argument about the great extent to which European imperialism disrupted or even destroyed some African economies.)
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of supporting an argument using a document's content — explicitly connects Doc 5's content to an argument about the great extent of economic disruption.

sample_responses.response: (Document 6): Ndansi Kumalo's account shows the devastation of traditional African economies by imperialism, by relating how the Ndebele were forced into wage work for the White settlers in mines and farms, where they were subjected to high taxes. (Connects the content of the document to an argument about the extent to which imperialism transformed and disrupted traditional African economic activities.)
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — uses Doc 6's content (Kumalo's account of forced wage work and taxation) to support a specific argument about imperialism's disruption of African economies.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 describes the government. Document 2 shows a protest.
sample_responses.commentary: Lists documents without using their content to support a specific argument about imperial economic impact — simply identifies topic rather than arguing.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Beyond Documents (1 pt): Uses at least one piece of specific historical evidence not found in the documents to support an argument. Must be specific — general background knowledge does not count. Must differ from contextualization.

required_elements: Specific named historical fact not in any of the seven documents
Used to support an argument about European imperialism's economic impact
Not the same as the contextualization evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Beyond the documents, the introduction of the head tax (hut tax) across British colonial Africa — for example, the 1898 Hut Tax War in Sierra Leone triggered by British imposition of a 5-shilling annual tax on every dwelling — demonstrates how colonial tax policy compelled subsistence farmers to engage in wage labor or commodity production to acquire colonial currency, fundamentally restructuring African economies by monetizing subsistence relationships.

required_elements: Specific outside evidence supporting the argument
Named event, policy, statistic, or institution not in the documents
alternatives.correct_example: The destruction of the Indian textile industry provides outside evidence for the extent of imperial economic disruption: British textile imports, protected by favorable tariffs that prevented reciprocal Indian tariffs on British goods, caused Indian cotton textile exports to collapse from approximately 25% of world exports in 1800 to under 4% by 1870. This deindustrialization — not referenced in any of the documents — shows that the economic impact of imperialism extended beyond labor coercion to systematic destruction of manufacturing capacity.

required_elements: Specific outside evidence not present in the documents
alternatives.correct_example: The Congo Reform Association, founded by E. D. Morel and Roger Casement in 1904 following Casement's report on Belgian Congo atrocities (related to but distinct from the testimony in Doc 5), provides outside evidence that the economic brutality of the Leopoldian rubber system reached international consciousness and eventually forced Belgium to take over the Congo from Leopold II in 1908 — showing that the economic impact of imperialism also generated political responses and reform movements.

wrong_examples: European imperialism also affected other areas not shown in the documents. (Too vague — must name a specific piece of evidence)
There was also colonialism in Southeast Asia. (Not specific — does not name an event, statistic, or policy)
common_traps: Using the same event as the contextualization — outside evidence must be distinct from the context provided earlier
Mentioning a general historical topic (e.g., 'the Industrial Revolution') rather than a specific fact, event, or statistic
Providing outside evidence that only describes what the documents already show rather than adding new information
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence (beyond that found in the documents) relevant to an argument about the prompt. CB-listed acceptable outside evidence includes: British investment projects (e.g., building of railroads in India and Africa); large-scale European-driven infrastructure projects (e.g., Suez Canal); economic modernization initiatives taken by Asian and African states (e.g., Meiji Restoration in Japan); forced expulsion of ethnic groups from traditional land (e.g., Herero genocide, displacement of Arab and Berber populations in French Algeria); use of military force to require Asian and African states to alter trading patterns (e.g., Opium Wars). The evidence must be different from the contextualization and must be more than a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: The British opium trade in China eventually led to the Opium Wars, during which Britain used its military to force China to agree to unequal trade agreements.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of evidence beyond the documents — names a specific event (Opium Wars) and explains its relevance to an argument about imperial economic impact.

sample_responses.response: Local elites often cooperated with European forces for their own benefit, even though those agreements did not necessarily benefit their subjects. For instance, Egypt cooperated with France and Britain to build the Suez Canal, but many Egyptian workers suffered and died during the construction.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — provides specific outside evidence (Suez Canal construction, elite cooperation) with explanation of relevance to an argument about imperial economic impact.

sample_responses.response: There were also other important factors in this period.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague and nonspecific — does not name a specific piece of evidence beyond the documents.

scoring_points.description: Sourcing / HAPP (1 pt): For at least two documents, explains how or why the historical situation, audience, purpose, or point of view is relevant to an argument. Must explain the relevance — merely identifying HAPP does not earn credit.

required_elements: HAPP analysis for at least two documents
Each analysis explains how/why the HAPP feature is relevant to an argument about economic impact
Not just identification of who wrote it or when
alternatives.correct_example: Doc 3 (Bankim Chandra Chatterjee, 1872): Chatterjee's point of view as a Western-educated Indian journalist writing for a Bengali audience shapes his argument in a way that is relevant to evaluating the extent of imperial economic impact. His defense of British colonialism's trade expansion and his criticism of Indian weavers for refusing to adapt their hereditary occupations reveals the perspective of an elite whose class position benefited from expanded commodity trade — meaning his dismissal of the weaving industry's collapse should be read as representing the views of a small commercial elite, not the majority of Indian artisans whose livelihoods were destroyed. This suggests Doc 3's apparent positive assessment of imperial economics reflects class bias rather than an accurate picture of colonial economic outcomes. Doc 5 (Moyo, 1904): The historical situation of this testimony — given to a Belgian government investigation in response to international scandal about Congo atrocities — shapes its purpose. Moyo's account was provided specifically to document abuses for an official inquiry, which means it was likely selected for its value as evidence of the most extreme abuses rather than a representative sample of colonial labor experiences, suggesting the document should be evaluated as demonstrating the upper bound of extractive brutality rather than the average experience.

required_elements: HAPP analysis for two different documents
Each explains how the HAPP feature affects the document's usefulness for arguing about imperial economic impact
alternatives.correct_example: Doc 4 (Lobengula Khumalo, 1888): The historical situation in which Lobengula signed this contract is critically relevant: by 1888, European powers were actively partitioning Africa and Rhodes' company was specifically seeking to acquire Ndebele territory before rivals could. This situation explains why the contract exists at all — Lobengula needed arms and money to defend against neighboring threats — but it also means the contract does not represent a free economic negotiation. The document's value for arguing the extent of economic disruption lies precisely in showing how African rulers were strategically maneuvered into granting economic concessions under geopolitical pressure rather than through genuine commercial exchange. Doc 7 (anonymous Tanzanian, 1967): The audience of this testimony — African historians from newly independent Tanzania — is relevant to its content. Because the interview was conducted by historians in the context of national independence, the audience likely encouraged the speaker to frame his account in terms of the injustice and exploitative nature of German rule, meaning the document is especially useful for documenting the lived economic experience of colonial labor but should be read with awareness that the post-independence context may have shaped the framing of the narrative.

wrong_examples: Doc 3 was written by an Indian journalist, which means his view is biased. (Identifies point of view but does not explain how the bias is relevant to the argument about imperial economic impact)
Doc 5 is a primary source from someone who experienced Belgian colonialism. (Identifies source type but provides no HAPP analysis or argument relevance)
common_traps: Identifying who wrote the document without explaining how that fact is relevant to the argument
Saying a document is 'biased' without explaining what the bias is and how it affects the document's usefulness for the argument
Applying HAPP to only one document — the rubric requires analysis of at least two
scoring_points.official_rubric: For at least three documents (CB 2022 standard), explains how or why the document's point of view, purpose, historical situation, and/or audience is relevant to an argument. The response must explain how or why — rather than simply identifying — the POV, purpose, situation, or audience is relevant to an argument that addresses the prompt for each of the documents sourced.

sample_responses.response: (Document 1): The author of Document 1 was a manager of a Dutch-government-owned sugar factory in Indonesia. As a manager, it would have been his duty to report to his superiors about the damage that the Dutch Cultivation System has inflicted on the availability of Indonesian labor for his factory. (Connects the point of view of the author to an argument about the negative influence of Dutch imperial policies on the economy of Java.)
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of acceptable POV analysis — explains how the author's position as a factory manager shapes the document's relevance to an argument about imperial economic impact.

sample_responses.response: (Document 7): The purpose of the account is to document the cruelty of the Germans and the economic hardships placed on Africans working on the cotton plantations. Because the account was given to African historians after Tanzania became independent, they would likely have wanted to emphasize the negative aspects of European imperialism.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of acceptable purpose analysis — explains how the post-independence context shapes the document's framing and its relevance to the argument.

sample_responses.response: (Document 4): The Ndebele ruler, whose territory was probably under a lot of pressure from White settlers as well as from neighboring African groups, likely thought that he had no choice but to trade away the right to mine raw materials in his country to the English in exchange for guns and a steamboat with which he could defend himself.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of acceptable historical situation analysis — explains how the geopolitical pressures on Lobengula shape the document's relevance to arguing about economic change in Africa.

sample_responses.response: The author of Document 5 was a victim of the violence in the Belgian Congo.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — identifies a feature of the source but does NOT explain how or why it is relevant to an argument.

scoring_points.description: Complexity (1 pt): Demonstrates a complex understanding of European imperialism's economic impact. Must be sustained throughout the essay — not a single sentence in the conclusion. Can be demonstrated through corroboration, cause-and-effect chains, comparison, or qualification.

required_elements: Sustained sophisticated argument throughout the essay
Not added as a final sentence — evident across multiple sections
alternatives.correct_example: A response earns complexity by arguing that the documents reveal a systematic progression: colonialism first coerced labor (Docs 1, 5, 7), then imposed taxes to perpetuate labor coercion even after formal quota systems were abandoned (Doc 6), then justified the entire system through the language of economic opportunity available to those willing to adapt (Doc 3). This pattern — from overt coercion to institutionalized tax-compelled wage labor to ideological justification — represents a multi-stage development in colonial economic control, not a single uniform impact. The complexity lies in showing how the mechanisms of economic control evolved within the colonial period itself, a pattern that must be woven through the essay from thesis to conclusion.

required_elements: Qualification, tension, or corroboration across the documents
Sustained throughout the essay, not a closing paragraph addition
alternatives.correct_example: A response earns complexity by showing that the documents present not just economic destruction but also the uneven distribution of harm within colonized societies. Doc 3's Indian intellectual defended British trade expansion because his class position benefited from it; Doc 4's Lobengula received cash and weapons even as his kingdom's mineral wealth was stripped; Doc 6's Ndebele recovered some livelihoods through wage labor before taxes extracted those gains. This pattern — where colonial economics created winners within colonized societies even as it systematically extracted wealth — must be traced throughout the essay to demonstrate that the extent of economic disruption was mediated by class, ethnicity, and position within colonial hierarchies.

wrong_examples: In conclusion, European imperialism had complex economic effects on Africa and Asia. (Single closing sentence — not sustained)
While some areas benefited, others were harmed by imperialism. (Vague acknowledgment of complexity without sustained, documented support)
common_traps: Adding one 'however' sentence at the end and claiming complexity — the sophistication must be demonstrated throughout
Describing multiple economic outcomes without explaining the deeper pattern connecting them
Treating complexity as simply mentioning counterevidence rather than explaining what it reveals about the limits of the main argument
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt, using evidence to corroborate, qualify, or modify an argument that addresses the question. CB-listed ways to demonstrate complexity include: analyzing multiple variables (e.g., exploring multiple economic impacts of European imperialism — physical exploitation of native labor, collapse of traditional economic activity, restructuring and realignment of elites, changes to gender roles); explaining relevant and insightful connections across time and space (e.g., comparing imperial effects in Docs 4 and 6 with settler colonialism in Australia or Algeria); corroborating multiple perspectives across themes. The demonstration must be part of the argument, not merely a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: A response may explore multiple economic impacts of European imperialism on native societies, including physical exploitation of native labor, collapse of traditional economic activity, restructuring and realignment of elites, changes to gender roles as a result of new economic demands, etc.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed pathway to complexity — analyzing multiple variables of imperial economic impact simultaneously (nuance). The student must actually execute this analysis throughout the essay.

sample_responses.response: A response may compare the effects of European imperialism seen in Documents 4 and 6 with the effects of imperialism in other colonial settings where large numbers of White settlers encroached on the territories of native peoples — such as in Australia or Algeria. Or a response may compare the exploitation of native labor for the extraction of resources, as seen in Documents 1, 5, and 7, with the exploitation of native labor through indirect imperialism in Latin America through forced labor systems such as indenture and debt servitude.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed pathway to complexity — explaining relevant and insightful connections across time and space by comparing settler-colonial or forced-labor dynamics across regions.

sample_responses.response: In conclusion, European imperialism had complex economic effects on Africa and Asia.
sample_responses.commentary: Single closing sentence — does not demonstrate sustained complexity through analysis of multiple variables or insightful connections as required by the CB rubric.
